title: Squeezed-slit Bohr-Einstein Interferometer
authors: Hao-Wen Cheng, Xu-Zhao-Qiu Zeng, Yu-Chen Zhang, Yu-Hao Deng, Zhan Wu, Rui Lin, Yu-Cheng Duan, Zi-Han Chen, Jun Rui, Ming-Cheng Chen, Chao-Yang Lu, Jian-Wei Pan
abstract: The Einstein-Bohr recoiling-slit gedankenexperiment, a cornerstone of quantum complementarity, has long been constrained by the zero-point fluctuations of the atomic slit -- the spatial Standard Quantum Limit (SQL). Here we transcend this fundamental boundary through active quantum state engineering of a single-atom slit. By implementing a non-adiabatic quench-evolve-quench protocol, we prepare the atomic motion in a squeezed state, dynamically redistributing phase-space uncertainty to suppress which-path information and restore high-visibility interference beyond the static vacuum limit. We report an intrinsic visibility of $0.938_{-0.008}^{+0.004}$, violating the SQL ($0.819$) by over 10 standard deviations, corresponding to $7.6(2)$ dB of effective squeezing. Our work reveals Kerr-induced non-Gaussian dynamics and reinterprets the traditional interferometer as a powerful tool for continuous-variable Wigner tomography, bridging the gap between quantum foundations and advanced metrology.
summary: The study demonstrates a method to surpass the Standard Quantum Limit in a single-atom interferometer by using a squeezed-state protocol. By engineering the motion of a trapped Rubidium atom, the researchers achieved interference visibility significantly higher than the vacuum limit. The work also shows that this setup can be used for high-fidelity Wigner function reconstruction.
main_problem: The Einstein-Bohr recoiling-slit experiment is fundamentally limited by the Standard Quantum Limit (SQL) due to zero-point fluctuations of the atomic slit. The goal is to transcend this limit by actively engineering the quantum state of the atomic slit.
main_result: The researchers achieved an intrinsic interference visibility of 0.938, violating the SQL of 0.819 by over 10 standard deviations, corresponding to 7.6 dB of effective squeezing.
method: A non-adiabatic quench-evolve-quench (QEQ) protocol is used to prepare the atom in a squeezed state, combined with global Bayesian analysis and Wigner tomography to reconstruct the quantum state.
model_or_system: A single 87Rb atom trapped in an 852-nm optical tweezer, subject to an effective Kerr Hamiltonian due to the intrinsic anharmonicity of the trap.
key_observables: Interference visibility (V), quantum characteristic function (chi), Wigner function (W), and photon count asymmetry.
important_parameters: Lamb-Dicke parameter (eta = 0.316), squeezing parameter (S1 = 0.50), Kerr coefficient (K/omega1 = -0.011), and trap frequencies (37.8 kHz to 12.8 kHz).
main_assumptions: The sudden quench approximation is used for trap modulation; the Truncated Wigner Approximation (TWA) is used to model non-Gaussian dynamics; and the system is assumed to be in the weak nonlinearity regime.
figures_summary: Figure 3 shows visibility as a function of trap durations and comparison to thermal limits; Figure 4 illustrates the evolution of the Wigner function from Gaussian to S-shaped non-Gaussian distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Bohr-Einstein problem, describes the experimental setup and QEQ protocol, presents the results of surpassing the SQL, analyzes the impact of Kerr-induced non-Gaussianity, and demonstrates the utility of the setup for Wigner tomography.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates active quantum state engineering to beat fundamental limits and provides a new interferometric method for continuous-variable Wigner tomography.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The paper directly studies the manipulation of the motional state (squeezing) of an atom to influence interference visibility, which is a central study of the correlation between external/motional degrees of freedom and the interference outcome.
